questions.stem: Which patient should the nurse assess first?

options.text: A patient with new chest pressure and nausea
A patient asking for a pillow
A patient wanting discharge paperwork
A patient asking for ice water

questions.rationale: New chest pressure with nausea may indicate acute cardiac ischemia and must be assessed immediately. **Why the others are wrong:** - B Comfort requests are not the top priority. - C Discharge teaching can wait. - D Water is not urgent compared with possible cardiac symptoms.

distractorRationales.b: A patient asking for a pillow misses the clinical pattern: Chest Pain Triage cardiac points to A patient with new chest pressure and nausea, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen circulation or perfusion.
distractorRationales.c: A patient wanting discharge paperwork misses the clinical pattern: Chest Pain Triage cardiac points to A patient with new chest pressure and nausea, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen circulation or perfusion.
distractorRationales.d: A patient asking for ice water misses the clinical pattern: Chest Pain Triage cardiac points to A patient with new chest pressure and nausea, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen circulation or perfusion.

questions.takeaway: Chest symptoms with nausea are high-priority until proven otherwise.

questions.stem: Which action best helps reduce transmission when caring for a patient with a contagious infection?

options.text: Perform appropriate hand hygiene before and after care
Reuse gloves if they still look clean
Skip precautions if the patient feels better
Share equipment without cleaning it

questions.rationale: Hand hygiene remains one of the most effective infection prevention measures.

distractorRationales.b: Reuse gloves if they still look clean misses the clinical pattern: Infection Prevention infection points to Perform appropriate hand hygiene before and after care, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen infection progression.
distractorRationales.c: Skip precautions if the patient feels better misses the clinical pattern: Infection Prevention infection points to Perform appropriate hand hygiene before and after care, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen infection progression.
distractorRationales.d: Share equipment without cleaning it misses the clinical pattern: Infection Prevention infection points to Perform appropriate hand hygiene before and after care, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen infection progression.

questions.takeaway: Simple infection-control steps still matter most.

questions.stem: Which patient should the nurse see first?

options.text: A patient with new chest pressure and diaphoresis
A patient needing help setting up a meal tray
A patient asking when rounds will begin
A patient who wants to know if family can visit later

questions.rationale: Chest pressure with diaphoresis can signal an acute cardiac emergency and must be assessed immediately. **Why the others are wrong:** - B Meal setup is not urgent. - C Rounds timing is nonurgent. - D Visiting questions can wait.

distractorRationales.b: A patient needing help setting up a meal tray misses the clinical pattern: New Deterioration cardiac points to A patient with new chest pressure and diaphoresis, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen circulation or perfusion.

distractorRationales.d: A patient who wants to know if family can visit later misses the clinical pattern: New Deterioration cardiac points to A patient with new chest pressure and diaphoresis, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen circulation or perfusion.

questions.takeaway: Acute chest symptoms outrank routine requests.

questions.stem: Which task is best for the nurse to delegate to AP?

options.text: Transporting a stable patient to radiology
Teaching a patient how to use an inhaler
Evaluating a new complaint of dizziness
Deciding whether chest pain needs an ECG

questions.rationale: Transporting a stable patient is a routine, predictable task that can be delegated when the nurse remains available for assessment follow-up. **Why the others are wrong:** - B Teaching requires nursing knowledge. - C Dizziness requires assessment first. - D Chest pain decisions require nursing judgment and escalation.

distractorRationales.b: Teaching a patient how to use an inhaler misses the clinical pattern: Safe Delegation pain points to Transporting a stable patient to radiology, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen circulation or perfusion.
distractorRationales.c: Evaluating a new complaint of dizziness misses the clinical pattern: Safe Delegation pain points to Transporting a stable patient to radiology, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen circulation or perfusion.
distractorRationales.d: Deciding whether chest pain needs an ECG misses the clinical pattern: Safe Delegation pain points to Transporting a stable patient to radiology, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen circulation or perfusion.

questions.takeaway: Delegate routine tasks for stable patients, not assessment or teaching.

A patient asking for a blanket

questions.stem: A patient suddenly becomes confused and pulls at lines. What should the nurse do first?

options.text: Assess the patient for a possible acute cause and protect safety
Assume the confusion is normal in the evening
Leave the patient alone to avoid upsetting them
Focus only on the charting

questions.rationale: Sudden confusion can signal an acute change. The nurse should protect the patient and look for the cause. **Why the others are wrong:** - B Sudden confusion should not be dismissed. - C Leaving the patient alone can increase harm. - D Charting does not come before assessment and safety.

distractorRationales.b: Assume the confusion is normal in the evening misses the clinical pattern: Acute Confusion assessment points to Assess the patient for a possible acute cause and protect safety, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen neurologic deterioration.
distractorRationales.c: Leave the patient alone to avoid upsetting them misses the clinical pattern: Acute Confusion assessment points to Assess the patient for a possible acute cause and protect safety, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen neurologic deterioration.
distractorRationales.d: Focus only on the charting misses the clinical pattern: Acute Confusion assessment points to Assess the patient for a possible acute cause and protect safety, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen neurologic deterioration.

questions.takeaway: New confusion should be treated as a change in condition, not routine behavior.

questions.stem: A patient becomes much less responsive after previously answering clearly. What is the nurse's first priority?

options.text: Reassess airway, breathing, circulation, and neurologic status
Wait for the patient to wake up naturally
Document that the patient is probably just tired
Offer the patient food before assessing further

questions.rationale: Sudden decreased responsiveness can reflect neurologic, respiratory, circulatory, or metabolic deterioration. **Why the others are wrong:** - B Delay may miss a serious change. - C Tiredness should not be assumed. - D Food does not replace an urgent assessment.

distractorRationales.b: Wait for the patient to wake up naturally misses the clinical pattern: Acute Level Change respiratory points to Reassess airway, breathing, circulation, and neurologic status, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.
distractorRationales.c: Document that the patient is probably just tired misses the clinical pattern: Acute Level Change respiratory points to Reassess airway, breathing, circulation, and neurologic status, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.
distractorRationales.d: Offer the patient food before assessing further misses the clinical pattern: Acute Level Change respiratory points to Reassess airway, breathing, circulation, and neurologic status, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.

questions.takeaway: Sudden decreased responsiveness is an emergency until proven otherwise.

questions.stem: A patient suddenly develops slurred speech and one-sided facial droop. What is the nurse's priority?

options.text: Activate the stroke response process immediately
Recheck the patient after the next medication pass
Ask the patient to rest and avoid talking
Document the change and continue routine care

questions.rationale: Sudden focal neurologic changes can signal stroke. Rapid response matters because time-sensitive treatment may be available. **Why the others are wrong:** - B Delay can cost critical treatment time. - C Rest does not address the neurologic emergency. - D Routine care is not appropriate when stroke is possible.

distractorRationales.b: Recheck the patient after the next medication pass misses the clinical pattern: Acute Neurologic Warning Sign stroke points to Activate the stroke response process immediately, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen neurologic deterioration.
distractorRationales.c: Ask the patient to rest and avoid talking misses the clinical pattern: Acute Neurologic Warning Sign stroke points to Activate the stroke response process immediately, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen neurologic deterioration.
distractorRationales.d: Document the change and continue routine care misses the clinical pattern: Acute Neurologic Warning Sign stroke points to Activate the stroke response process immediately, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen neurologic deterioration.

questions.takeaway: Sudden slurred speech and facial droop are stroke warning signs.

questions.stem: A patient with asthma initially improves after treatment, but later becomes quieter, less able to speak, and more fatigued. What is the best interpretation?

options.text: The patient may be tiring and failing to sustain respiratory effort
The quieter breathing proves the patient is improving
Fatigue is unrelated if the lungs were wheezing earlier
The nurse can wait until the next round to reassess

questions.rationale: A quieter patient who is more fatigued can actually be worsening. Less noise is not always better when work of breathing is falling off because the patient is tiring. **Why the others are wrong:** - B Less sound can mean less effort, not improvement. - C Earlier wheezing does not rule out later fatigue. - D This pattern needs prompt reassessment.

distractorRationales.b: The quieter breathing proves the patient is improving misses the clinical pattern: Airway Change After Treatment airway points to The patient may be tiring and failing to sustain respiratory effort, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.
distractorRationales.c: Fatigue is unrelated if the lungs were wheezing earlier misses the clinical pattern: Airway Change After Treatment airway points to The patient may be tiring and failing to sustain respiratory effort, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.
distractorRationales.d: The nurse can wait until the next round to reassess misses the clinical pattern: Airway Change After Treatment airway points to The patient may be tiring and failing to sustain respiratory effort, so choosing it can postpone safety actions and worsen oxygenation or ventilation.

questions.takeaway: Less audible effort can be a bad sign if the patient is tiring.
